title: Better by Default: Strong Pre-Tuned MLPs and Boosted Trees on Tabular Data (RealMLP)
authors: David Holzmüller, Léo Grinsztajn, Ingo Steinwart (University of Stuttgart; Inria/Soda)
venue: NeurIPS 2024 (arXiv:2407.04491, submitted Jul 2024, final rev Jan 2025)
tokenization_method: RealMLP contributes NO new attention-style tokenizer; it is a flat-concatenation MLP with a carefully engineered per-feature embedding+preprocessing front-end ('bag of tricks'). Numerical features: robust (IQR) scaling -> smooth clipping f(x)=x/(1+(|x|/3)^2) into ~(-3,3) -> optional PBLD numerical embedding (periodic-bias-linear-densenet: per-feature 2-layer MLP producing (x_i, W^(2,i) cos(2*pi*w^(1,i) x_i + b^(1,i)) + b^(2,i)) in R^4, i.e. original value concatenated to a 3-dim periodic embedding from a 16-dim cosine basis). Categorical: one-hot for cardinality <=8 (binary mapped to {-1,1}, missing->0); learned 8-dim embedding for cardinality >8 (missing as its own category). After per-feature encoding, a LEARNABLE DIAGONAL SCALING LAYER (per-feature scalar s_i init 1.0) rescales inputs before the first linear layer; everything is then flattened/concatenated into one wide vector fed to a 3-hidden-layer (256-wide) MLP with NTP parametrization. Token granularity is therefore per-feature embedding, immediately flattened — no token sequence, no [CLS], no self-attention.
feature_types_handled: numerical and categorical (mixed). Numerical via robust-scale + smooth-clip + optional PBLD periodic embedding; categorical via one-hot (<=8) or learned 8-dim embedding (>8); binary -> {-1,1}; missing categorical -> separate category. No native missing-value handling for NUMERICAL features (paper explicitly restricts to datasets with no missing numerical values). No native temporal/time-varying handling — rows are static unordered feature vectors.
token_granularity: per-feature embedding then FLATTENED/CONCATENATED into one input vector (not a retained token sequence). Each numerical feature -> 4-dim PBLD embedding (or scalar if embeddings off); each small-cardinality categorical -> one-hot block; each high-cardinality categorical -> 8-dim learned embedding. Feature mixing happens inside the shared dense weight matrices, not via per-token attention.
numerical_strategy: Robust scaling using the inter-quartile range, then a smooth clipping function f(x)=x/(1+(|x|/3)^2) mapping into (-3,3) (chosen over sklearn QuantileTransformer; ablated as a win). Optionally followed by PBLD ('periodic bias linear densenet') numerical embeddings: a per-feature small MLP whose periodic (cosine) branch uses learnable frequency w in R^16 and bias in R^16, projected to R^3 and concatenated with the original value -> R^4 per feature. PBLD reported ~+0.5% over plain piecewise-linear (PL) embeddings on regression. RealMLP-TD-S (simplified) drops embeddings entirely.
categorical_strategy: Threshold at 8 distinct values. Cardinality <=8: one-hot encoding (binary feature -> single {-1,1} channel; missing -> zero vector). Cardinality >8: learned 8-dimensional embedding table initialized N(0,1), with missing encoded as a dedicated extra category. This is an explicit, simple high-vs-low-cardinality switch baked into the default preprocessing.
high_cardinality_handling: Explicit but minimal: any categorical with >8 distinct values gets a learned 8-dim embedding (fixed dim regardless of cardinality), avoiding one-hot blow-up. There is NO target/CatBoost encoding, NO hashing, NO frequency-aware sizing — the embedding dim is a constant 8. For very high-cardinality fields (thousands of categories) this fixed-8-dim table is a heuristic that the paper does not stress-test; meta-test datasets go up to ~10K features (mostly via one-hot/expanded columns) but the paper does not isolate high-cardinality categorical performance.
temporal_handling: none. No patching, recurrence, channel-as-token, or temporal-static fusion. Datasets are consumed as i.i.d. static rows; any time structure must be feature-engineered upstream.
scales_to_many_features: Linear in feature count by construction (flat-concat MLP, no O(k^2) self-attention). Meta-train datasets reach up to 561 features (classification) / 520 (regression); meta-test datasets up to ~10,000 (classification) / ~4,991 (regression) features and 1K-500K samples. The paper's headline contribution is a favorable TIME-accuracy tradeoff vs other neural nets, and it runs without per-dataset tuning. Caveat: 'many features' on meta-test is largely one-hot-expanded width rather than a controlled sweep of 70+ native heterogeneous fields; per-feature PBLD embeddings multiply the input width by ~4x for numericals.
downstream_model: MLP: 3 hidden layers of width 256 with neural tangent parametrization (NTP), parametric activations (a learnable blend (1-alpha)x + alpha*sigma(x); SELU for classification, Mish for regression; alpha init 1.0), dropout (base 0.15, flat_cos scheduled), AdamW (beta2=0.95 not 0.999), multi-cycle coslog4 LR schedule, scheduled weight decay (base 0.02), label smoothing 0.1 (classification), test-time output clipping to training range (regression), 256 epochs with best-validation-epoch revert (no early stopping). Data-dependent weight init (unit pre-activation variance) + he+5 bias init.
pretraining_objective: none for the model itself. The KEY meta-level objective is META-TUNING OF DEFAULTS: hyperparameters are optimized (hyperopt/SMAC3 + small manual rounding) on a meta-train benchmark of 118 datasets (71 cls + 47 reg, UCI) to produce ONE fixed 'tuned default' (TD) config, then frozen and evaluated on a disjoint meta-test benchmark of 90 datasets (48 cls + 42 reg, AMLB + OpenML-CTR23). Same recipe applied to GBDTs to get LightGBM-TD / XGBoost-TD / CatBoost-TD. Scoring uses shifted geometric mean error (SGM, eps=0.01).
key_results: On the disjoint meta-test benchmark (90 datasets), RealMLP-TD (a single fixed config, no per-dataset tuning) is competitive with CatBoost-TD and clearly beats tuned-default MLP-D and ResNet-D; win rate of RealMLP-TD vs CatBoost-TD is ~51-62% across benchmarks. RealMLP-HPO (50 random-search steps) outperforms individual HPO'd GBDTs. The 'Best-TD' meta-model (pick best of RealMLP-TD/CatBoost-TD/LGBM-TD/XGB-TD per dataset) beats any single 50-step HPO method while being faster. On the GBDT-friendly Grinsztajn et al. (2022) benchmark, RealMLP is on par with or better than other NNs (FT-Transformer, SAINT) and comparable to GBDTs under HPO, but 'worse for classification, comparable for regression'. Ablation magnitudes: removing beta2=0.95 costs ~+22.8% regression error; removing parametric activations ~+4.8% regression; learnable scaling layer ~+1.2%; data-dependent init ~+1%; PBLD over PL ~+0.5% regression; label smoothing ~+1.8% classification. 5-fold refit ensembling gives ~7.7-8.9% extra classification error reduction; refitting beats bagging.
baselines_compared: GBDTs: LightGBM, XGBoost, CatBoost (each at default-D, tuned-default-TD, and HPO). Neural: tuned MLP-D, ResNet-D, FT-Transformer, SAINT, MLP-PLR-style embedding baselines. Internal: RealMLP-TD vs RealMLP-TD-S (simplified) vs RealMLP-HPO; cumulative 'bag of tricks' ablation ladder over a plain MLP.
transferable_idea: For 70+ heterogeneous features you can often skip the Transformer: a flat-concat MLP with (a) robust-scale + smooth-clip numerical preprocessing, (b) per-feature PBLD periodic embeddings, (c) a learnable per-feature DIAGONAL SCALING layer before the first linear (implicit feature-importance reweighting, ~+1.2%), and (d) a strong fixed training recipe, matches or beats attention tokenizers at LINEAR cost. The single most portable artifact is the META-TUNED DEFAULT methodology: tune one config on a meta-train suite, freeze it, and ship strong out-of-the-box per-feature embeddings + preprocessing so a downstream pipeline needs no per-dataset HPO.
limitations: Authors-stated: (a) datasets contain NO missing numerical values and sizes restricted to 1K-500K samples; (b) only one train-validation split per train-test split (no cross-validation), so HPO can overfit validation; (c) non-architectural training aspects are NOT equalized across NN baselines, muddying clean architecture comparisons; (d) runtimes depend on parallelization / HPO algorithm / hardware; (e) authors concede 'it is unclear what the current best deep tabular model is'. Topic-relevant gaps: high-cardinality embedding dim fixed at 8 with no target/hashing encoding; no temporal/time-series fusion; no explicit feature-interaction or feature-selection layer (the diagonal scaling layer is only per-feature reweighting, not interaction); slower per-epoch than GBDTs (~1 order of magnitude).
pitfalls: 1) PRIMARY-ROUTE MISMATCH: filed under feature-interaction-selection, but RealMLP does NOT model explicit feature interactions (no cross/attention layer) and the learnable scaling layer is per-feature reweighting, not selection. Its only tie to the route is linear-cost handling of many features. 2) NOT a tokenizer in the attention sense — the contribution is a preprocessing+embedding+training recipe, not a new token representation; PBLD embeddings are an incremental variant of PLR/PL embeddings (Gorishniy 2022). Disentangle credit: much of the gain is the training recipe (beta2=0.95, parametric activations) NOT the embedding. 3) The 'competitive with GBDTs' headline rests on shifted-geometric-mean scoring and on the TUNED-DEFAULT regime; CatBoost-TD is within ~one win-rate point and RealMLP is 'worse for classification' on the GBDT-friendly benchmark. 4) High-cardinality handling is a constant 8-dim embedding with no frequency awareness — untested on thousands-of-categories fields. 5) 'Many features' on meta-test is largely one-hot-expanded width, not a controlled 70+-native-field sweep. 6) No missing-numerical support and no temporal handling — both required by this topic's target setting.
relevance_to_topic: High as a strong, cheap, linear-cost MLP backbone + a proven default per-feature numerical-embedding/preprocessing recipe to drop a heterogeneous-feature tokenizer onto; medium-low as a tokenizer itself (no attention, fixed-8-dim high-cardinality, no temporal/missing-numeric support).
code_available: yes — official pytabkit (https://github.com/dholzmueller/pytabkit), scikit-learn-style fit/predict, exposes RealMLP (TD/HPO/Ensemble) and XGB/LGBM/CatBoost (D/TD/HPO); actively maintained (v1.7.3 Jan 2026, ~371 stars).
